stocks.name: Breeding Stock
stocks.comment: Sows and gilts held for breeding.

stocks.name: Inventory
stocks.comment: Inventory of frozen and cured pork.

stocks.name: Mature Stock
stocks.comment:
"Hogs in the terminal stage of feeding and preparation either for slaughter or for 				 addition
		         to the breeding stock." (Meadows, p.54).

stocks.name: Piglets In Utero
stocks.comment: Pig fetuses that will survive birth and weening.

variables.name: normal consumption
variables.comment: normal consumption

variables.name: acquiring breeding stock
variables.comment:
Net rate at which sows and gilts are either acquired for breeding or returned to 				 mature
		        stock for slaughtering. A positive rate indicates more sows and gilts are 				 being acquired
		        for breeding than are being returned to feeding pens for slaughter. A 				 negative number
		        indicates more are being returned for slaughter than are being added to the 				 breeding
		        stock. Note: The basic policy is (Desired Breeding Stock-Breeding 				 Stock)/Breeding Stock
		         Acquisition Delay. The complication keeps the level of mature 				 hogs from going negative.
variables.equation: IF( desired_breeding_stock > Breeding_Stock , IF( Mature_Stock > TIME_STEP * ( desired_breeding_stock - Breeding_Stock ) / breeding_stock_acquisition_delay , ( desired_breeding_stock - Breeding_Stock ) / breeding_stock_acquisition_delay , Mature_Stock / TIME_STEP ) , ( desired_breeding_stock - Breeding_Stock ) / breeding_stock_acquisition_delay )

variables.name: breeding
variables.comment:
Rate at which pigs are created in utero. This rate is net of any pigs that do not 				 survive to
		         maturity.

variables.name: breeding stock acquisition delay
variables.comment:
Dealy for farmers to adjust their breeding stocks to desired levels. Source: 				 Meadows' Hog
		         Cycle model (BSAD)

variables.name: consuming
variables.comment: National consumption of pork
variables.equation: IF( Inventory + slaughtering * TIME_STEP > Per_Capita_Consumption * population * TIME_STEP , Per_Capita_Consumption * population , Inventory / TIME_STEP )

variables.name: coverage effect on price
variables.comment: Effect that surplus or deficient pork inventories have on the price of 				 live hogs.

variables.name: desired breeding stock
variables.comment: Number of sows and gilts farmers would like to have for breeding.

variables.name: desired coverage
variables.comment: Desired months of pork inventory. Source: Meadows (DCOV)

variables.name: effect of price on consumption
variables.comment: Impact of retail price on national consumption of pork.

variables.name: expected corn price
variables.comment: Farmers' expected price of corn. Source: Meadows (FECCP)

variables.name: Expected Hog Price
variables.comment: The price farmers expect to receive for live hogs.

variables.name: farrowing
variables.comment: Hog birth rate.

variables.name: fertility
variables.comment:
The fertility of hogs after accounting for hogs lost at birth, weaning or 				 elswewhere before
		        slaughter or breeding. Source: Meadows: Litters per hog * pigs saved per 				 litter * weaning
		         survival factor =LPHM*PSPL*WSF = .17*7 *.7=0.83

variables.name: gestation time
variables.comment: Average pregnancy period for hogs. Source: Meadows, figure 5-14

variables.name: hog corn price ratio
variables.comment:
The ratio of the price of a hog to the price of a bushel of corn. The "exchange 				 rate" of a
		         hog in terms of bushels of corn.

variables.name: hog dressing yield
variables.comment: Fraction of each hog that is not discarded as waste during dressing. 				 Source: Meadows (DY)

variables.name: hog price
variables.comment: The price farmers receive for live hogs.

variables.name: indicated per capita consumption
variables.comment:
Per capita consumption that would hold if the retail price had worked its way 				 through the
		         system, and if people had had time to completely react to it.

variables.name: input
variables.comment:
Test input that disturbs the system from an initial equilibrium. The test input is 				 designed
		        to elicit the inherent dynamics of the system. The test input does not 				 represent an
		         historical event.

variables.name: inventory coverage
variables.comment: Months of inventory at the current consumption rate

variables.name: live weight of slaughtered hogs
variables.comment: Weight of fully mature hogs, ready for slaughter. Source: Meadows (LWSH)

variables.name: marketing margin
variables.comment:
Marketing Margin in Meadows is $28/100 lbs. A hog is 240 lbs, and the dressing 				 yield in .58.
		        So the margin is $39/hog. However, this leads to unstable (or undamped) 				 oscillations,
		        because of the delay in consumption (which is not in the original model) so 				 we go with
		         $30/hog.

variables.name: mature stock feeding period
variables.comment: Average time hogs are held (and fed) after becoming mature. Source: 				 Meadows, figure 5-14.

variables.name: maturing
variables.comment: Rate at which piglets becoming mature hogs.

variables.name: normal breeding stock
variables.comment:
The normal number of sows and gilts held for breeding. This is the equilibrium 				 quantity,
		        given the normal hog/corn price ratio. Source: Meadows (BSTAB). Note: the 				 normal hog
		         dcorn price ratio is about 20 bushels per hog.

variables.name: normal hog corn price ratio
variables.comment: The equilibrium hog corn price ratio.

variables.name: normal hog price
variables.comment: The initial normal hog price.

variables.name: normal price per pounds
variables.comment:
The initial normal price per pound of live hogs. Source: From meadows HPTAB, this 				 is the
		         price per lbs when RCOV = 1. (In Meadows, the price is 16.5 per 				 hundred pound.

variables.name: normal retail price
variables.comment: The initial normal retail price of pork.

variables.name: Per Capita Consumption
variables.comment: Pork consumption per person per month in the U.S.

variables.name: population
variables.comment: U.S. population. Source: Meadows (POP)

variables.name: price effect on breeding stock
variables.comment:
Impact of relative hog/corn price ratio on desired breeding stock. As the relative 				 hog/corn
		        price ratio increases, farmers desire to increase their production, and 				 hence to increase
		         breeding stock.

variables.name: relative coverage
variables.comment: Months of pork inventory, relative to desired.

variables.name: Relative Hog Corn Ratio
variables.comment: The hog corn price ratio relative to normal

variables.name: relative hog price
variables.comment: Retail price of pork, relative to normal retail price

variables.name: retail price
variables.comment: Retail price of pork.

variables.name: slaughtering
variables.comment: Hog slaughter rate.

variables.name: time to change consumption
variables.comment:
Average for pork consumption to react to a change in price. Represents perception 				 and
		        reaction delays, as well as the time it takes for a wholesale price increase 				 to work its
		        way through the system. This constant does not appear in Meadows. Source: 				 LeapTec
		         estimate.

variables.name: time to form price expectations
variables.comment:
Average time for farmers to adjust their expectations to a change in the level of 				 live-hog
		         prices. Source: Meadows (FEHPAD)

variables.name: time to mature
variables.comment:
Average time for a pig to reach maturity. The average period from Farrowing to 				 Maturity.
		        Includes time from Farrowing to Weaning (2 months), Weaning to First Heat (1 				 month), and
		         from First Heat to Maturity (2 months). Source: Meadows, figure 				 5-14.

lookupTables.name: coverage effect on price func
lookupTables.comment:
Functional relationship between pork relative pork inventory coverage and its 				 effect on the
		        price of live-hogs. Source: Meadows (HPTAB). Normalized around 16.5, the 				 coverage effect
		        for a relative coverage of 1. Meadow's table goes to 0 at rcov=2; the above 				 table does
		         not.

lookupTables.name: effect of price on consumption func
lookupTables.comment:
Functional relationship between relative price of pork and its impact on per capita
		        consumption. Source: Meadows PCCP. We have assumed the "Normal" retail price 				 is 60, and
		         so the normal consumption per capita is 5/person/month

lookupTables.name: price effect on breeding stock function
lookupTables.comment:
Functional relationship between relative Hog/Corn price ratio and its impact on 				 desired
		        breeding stock. Source: Meadows(BSTAB). We have normalized in the following 				 way. The
		        normal expected hog corn ratio is about 20 bushels/hog. The normal breeding 				 stock (from
		         the table, then, is about 9 million hogs.

comments.name: Comment 1
comments.text: Copyright (c) 1994 LeapTec and Ventana.  After Meadows. 1970. Dynamics of Commodity Production Cycles.

elements.name: effect of price on consumption func
price effect on breeding stock function

connectors.from: effect of price on consumption func
price effect on breeding stock function
